quick_facts.label: Typical entry designation
quick_facts.value: Agricultural Field Officer (Scale I), a specialist-officer post in participating public-sector banks under IBPS CRP-SPL.

quick_facts.label: Relevant degree route
quick_facts.value: The CRP-SPL XVI notification lists a four-year degree in Agriculture among the qualifying degrees for Agricultural Field Officer (Scale I). A B.Sc. (Hons.) Agriculture route is therefore relevant where it meets the notified four-year-degree requirement; applicants must check the notification applicable to their recruitment cycle.

quick_facts.label: Selection route
quick_facts.value: IBPS CRP-SPL XVI describes preliminary and main online examinations, followed by interview and provisional allotment for specialist-officer posts; the dates and process are recruitment-cycle specific.

quick_facts.label: Credit domain
quick_facts.value: The role sits in agricultural and rural lending: Kisan Credit Card lending can require assessment of cropping pattern, acreage, scale of finance, repayment period, documentation and security.

quick_facts.label: Sector context
quick_facts.value: Agricultural and rural credit supports the broader statutory objective of promoting agriculture and allied rural economic activity through credit and other facilities.

sections.heading: What the role is about
paragraphs: An Agricultural Field Officer applies agriculture knowledge within bank credit work. In practice, the work is centered on evaluating, processing and monitoring agriculture and allied-activity finance in accordance with the employer bank’s policy, delegated powers and applicable rules. The exact allocation of sanctioning, appraisal, customer acquisition and post-disbursement work varies by bank and posting.

Agricultural lending illustrates the technical inputs involved: SBI’s Kisan Credit Card product uses cropping pattern, acreage and district scale of finance to determine need-based credit, and specifies crop/asset hypothecation, possible land or property collateral, repayment linked to crop and marketing periods, and borrower documentation. These are examples of the lending context an AFO needs to understand, rather than a universal job-description checklist.
bullets: Interpret farm and allied-enterprise proposals using agronomy, local production conditions and bank credit criteria.
Support agricultural credit appraisal and documentation, including landholding, cropping-pattern and security-related information where applicable.

Work across crop credit, investment credit and allied activities; SBI’s rural-banking training catalogue includes investment credit, farmer-producer organisations, dairy and poultry finance, and asset-quality/NPA management.
Contribute to rural banking and financial-inclusion activity alongside branch, credit-processing and regional teams.

sections.heading: Entry route from B.Sc. (Hons.) Agriculture
paragraphs: The principal national recruitment route is the Institute of Banking Personnel Selection (IBPS) Common Recruitment Process for Specialist Officers (CRP-SPL), which includes Agricultural Field Officer (Scale I) among its participating-public-sector-bank specialist posts. Recruitment is not automatic on completion of the degree: candidates must satisfy the particular notification’s eligibility conditions and compete in the prescribed selection process.

For CRP-SPL XVI, the official notification identifies Agricultural Field Officer as a Scale I post, specifies a four-year degree in Agriculture among the eligible qualifications, and sets the indicative sequence of preliminary examination, main examination, interview and provisional allotment. The same notification gives a 20–30 age band for this post, subject to its stated relaxation provisions. All eligibility, vacancies, participating banks, fees and dates should be checked in the notification current when applying.
bullets: Complete an eligible four-year agriculture degree; retain marksheets, degree evidence and other documents required for verification.
Apply through the official IBPS recruitment portal during the notified registration window.
Prepare for both the general banking aptitude components and the professional-knowledge assessment specified for the relevant AFO recruitment cycle.
Treat provisional allotment as subject to the recruiting bank’s and IBPS’s verification conditions.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: The role needs a combination of agricultural-domain judgment and credit-process discipline. Useful technical capability includes understanding crop cycles, acreage and production-cost assumptions, farm and allied-activity economics, documentation, security, repayment capacity and risks affecting repayment. This follows from the design of agricultural credit products, where loan assessment can depend on crop pattern, land area, district scale of finance, security and crop/marketing-cycle repayment.

Banking skills matter alongside agricultural knowledge. SBI’s rural-banking training explicitly focuses on agriculture and allied activities, rural banking, microfinance, financial inclusion and field-based learning; its agriculture curriculum includes investment credit, farmer-producer organisations and asset-quality/NPA management. Clear communication with farmers and rural enterprises, documentation accuracy, ethical handling of credit decisions and comfort working with local stakeholders are consequently important.
bullets: Agronomy and agricultural economics, including crop and allied-enterprise viability.
Credit appraisal, financial analysis, loan documentation and security awareness.
Knowledge of rural-credit products, financial inclusion and government-linked agriculture schemes as applicable to the bank.
Field communication, local-context awareness, record keeping and digital banking workflow.

sections.heading: Where and how AFOs work
paragraphs: AFOs work within the branch-and-credit infrastructure of the bank rather than in a purely agricultural-extension setting. Potential assignments can involve rural or semi-urban branches, agricultural-credit functions, loan-processing or regional-office support, depending on the bank’s operating model and staffing needs.

The work connects desk-based credit processing with rural customers and field realities. SBI describes rural banking, financial inclusion and agriculture/allied-activity capability building that uses case studies, demo plots and field visits; it also records officers moving between Agri Field Officer, branch-management and other banking assignments. This supports an expectation of a mixed office-and-field professional environment, but not a guarantee of a particular posting for every recruit.
bullets: Possible interfaces include farmers, tenant cultivators, joint-liability groups, self-help groups, farmer organisations and branch colleagues.
Travel and field contact may be necessary for local business development, appraisal support or monitoring, subject to bank policy and posting.
Posting location and transfer conditions are determined by the allotted bank, not by IBPS alone.

sections.heading: Career development
paragraphs: Agricultural Field Officer is recruited at Scale I in the IBPS specialist-officer process. Subsequent progression is governed by the employing bank’s service rules, performance systems, vacancies, promotion policy and transfer requirements, so no single promotion timetable applies across all participating banks.

The specialist foundation can lead to broader agricultural-credit, rural-banking, credit, branch-management or regional roles. SBI’s own training material gives examples of officers who have served as Agri Field Officer and subsequently held branch-manager, SME, advances, credit/NPA or regional roles; these are illustrations of possible mobility, not promised career paths.
bullets: Build depth in agricultural and allied-sector credit, investment finance and portfolio quality.
Pursue bank-provided training and relevant banking qualifications where offered or required.
Develop broader operational and credit-management capability to be competitive for internal role changes and promotion.
